overall.summary: Graph+MCP (c) wins decisively: it computes impact by traversing the dependency graph, so it leads on completeness, contract precision, and determinism, and never over-claims. Markdown (a) is the most readable and never invents impact, but misses non-local effects (consumers of changed events, callers of changed APIs). Graph-RAG (b) sits in the middle — graph expansion recovers the edges Markdown misses, but it over-expands on single-service changes and its contracts are paraphrased.

evidence: graph-mcp: get_dependents('topic:order.created') -> [inventory-service]; names it as the consumer that must accept reservedQty (AC3).
graph-rag: the retrieved order.created chunk lists producer order-service and consumer inventory-service, so the consumer is surfaced.
markdown: hedges the consumer ('inventory-service's page also says it consumes order.created, so it may need to handle the new field — please confirm') — per the rubric a hedge counts as not-claimed.
markdown: never claims an unaffected service; on single-service tasks (KB-202 quantity cap, KB-205 cache TTL) it correctly stays within order-service / inventory-service respectively.
graph-mcp: returns no cross-service dependents for isolated changes — also correctly scoped.
graph-rag: 1-hop graph expansion drags the neighbouring service into single-service changes (KB-202, KB-205) — a false positive each.
graph-mcp: exact schemas from get_api — CreateOrderRequest{customerId,sku,quantity} -> OrderResponse(+reservationId), plus ReserveRequest/ReserveResponse.
graph-rag: schemas paraphrased from chunk text ('create-order request -> order response') — not exact.
markdown: request/response described in words only, no schema names or examples.
graph-mcp: a reverse-graph lookup returns the identical impact set on every run.
graph-rag: retrieval depends on k and query phrasing; the surfaced set can vary.
markdown: depends entirely on which pages the reader chooses to open.
markdown: plain prose a human reads instantly, zero infrastructure.
graph-mcp / graph-rag: structured and precise, but require a running MCP server to produce.

risks: markdown: low recall on non-local impact — misses consumers of changed topics (KB-203) and callers of changed APIs (KB-204); the gap widens at 40 services.
graph-rag: over-expansion adds false positives on single-service changes; contracts are approximate; retrieval is non-deterministic.
graph-mcp: requires a built graph and a running MCP server (operational cost) — the price of the deterministic win.
